items.title: When to Consider Temporary Pacemaker Use for Sinus Node Disease
items.content: In patients with symptomatic sinus node disease, initial management focuses on identifying and reversing any reversible causes such as medications or electrolyte imbalances. If these interventions are insufficient or if the medication causing bradycardia is necessary, temporary pacing may be indicated.
keyPoints: Reversible causes include acute myocardial infarction, electrolyte abnormalities, medications (e.g., beta blockers, calcium channel blockers, digoxin), and hypothyroidism.
Temporary pacing may be needed if medication causing bradycardia is unavoidable.
Bradycardia due to ischemic injury or drug accumulation post-heart transplantation might require temporary pacing until SA nodal recovery.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · TEMPORARY PACING FOR TRANSIENT SUPPORT

items.title: Management of Bradycardia Post-Spinal Cord Injury and Cardiac Surgery
items.content: Bradycardia following spinal cord injury or cardiac surgery can be managed with temporary pacing. Enhanced parasympathetic tone after spinal cord injury may necessitate atropine, inotropes, or adenosine blockade. After cardiac surgery, if the SA nodal artery is injured, temporary pacing or beta-1 adrenergic agonists might be required.
keyPoints: Post-spinal cord injury bradycardia can result from enhanced parasympathetic tone and autonomic dysreflexia.
Common triggers include tracheal suctioning and patient turning; management may involve atropine, inotropes, or adenosine blockade with theophylline/aminophylline.
Post-cardiac surgery bradycardia due to SA nodal artery injury might require temporary pacing or beta-1 adrenergic agonists.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · TEMPORARY PACING FOR TRANSIENT SUPPORT

items.title: Indications for Permanent Pacemaker in SA Nodal Disease
items.content: Permanent pacemakers (PPM) are indicated primarily to alleviate symptoms of bradycardia associated with sinus node dysfunction (SND). The decision is based on the correlation between symptoms and bradycardia. PPM is not typically recommended for asymptomatic sinus bradycardia or sleep-related pauses due to high vagal tone without other symptoms.
keyPoints: Pacing in SA nodal disease alleviates symptoms of bradycardia.
No established heart rate threshold below which pacemaker treatment is indicated; well-conditioned athletes may have resting rates <40 bpm.
Permanent pacing is not indicated for asymptomatic sinus bradycardia or sleep-related pauses due to high vagal tone without other symptoms.
Medications like methylxanthines or beta agonists are rarely used to avoid PPM, except temporarily in cases of active infection.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION

items.title: Approaches and Considerations for Pacemaker Implantation
items.content: PPM is the principal treatment for SND. Transvenous implantation through subclavian veins into cardiac chambers is most common, with leads attached to a generator placed in the pectoral region. Epicardial placement via surgical approaches like sternotomy or thoracotomy is less common and often combined with other procedures.
keyPoints: PPM is the principal treatment for SND, driven by symptom-bradycardia correlation.
Transvenous implantation through subclavian veins into cardiac chambers is most common.
Pacing leads can be placed epicardially via surgical approaches such as sternotomy or thoracotomy, often combined with other procedures.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: TABLE 251-2 Indications for Permanent Pacing in Sinus Node Dysfunction (SND)
items.title: Indications for Permanent Pacing in SND
items.content: Permanent pacing is indicated in patients with symptomatic sinus node dysfunction when symptoms are directly attributable to the condition, particularly when they result from essential medication therapy that cannot be altered.
keyPoints: Symptoms directly attributable to SND warrant consideration of permanent pacing.
Symptomatic sinus bradycardia due to necessary medications without alternatives is an indication for pacing.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · TABLE 251-2 Indications for Permanent Pacing in Sinus Node Dysfunction (SND)

items.subtopic: TABLE 251-2 Indications for Permanent Pacing in Sinus Node Dysfunction (SND)
items.title: Management of Tachy-brady Syndrome and Chronotropic Incompetence
items.content: In cases of tachy-brady syndrome or chronotropic incompetence where symptoms are potentially due to SND, a trial with oral theophylline may be considered to assess if permanent pacing is beneficial.
keyPoints: Tachy-brady syndrome and symptomatic chronotropic incompetence are indications for considering permanent pacing.
A trial of oral theophylline can help determine the need for permanent pacing by increasing heart rate.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · TABLE 251-2 Indications for Permanent Pacing in Sinus Node Dysfunction (SND)

items.question: Which of the following medications is NOT commonly associated with causing bradycardia?
items.explanation: Aspirin is not listed as a common medication causing bradycardia in the passage.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · TEMPORARY PACING FOR TRANSIENT SUPPORT

items.question: In patients with tachy-brady syndrome, what can prevent bradycardia events?
items.explanation: The passage states that alleviating the tachycardia can prevent bradycardia events.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · TEMPORARY PACING FOR TRANSIENT SUPPORT

items.question: What is a common cause of sinus bradycardia after heart transplantation?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that accumulated drugs such as amiodarone can affect the donor heart, causing sinus bradycardia.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · TEMPORARY PACING FOR TRANSIENT SUPPORT

items.question: Which of the following statements about permanent pacemaker implantation for SA nodal disease is correct?
options: Permanent pacing is typically indicated for sleep-related pauses due to high vagal tone in the absence of other symptoms.
Asymptomatic sinus bradycardia is commonly associated with adverse outcomes and warrants permanent pacing.
Medications like methylxanthines are frequently used to improve heart rate and avoid pacemaker implantation.
Permanent pacemaker implantation is primarily indicated when there is a strong correlation between symptoms and bradycardia.
items.explanation: PPM is the principal treatment for SND, especially when symptoms correlate with bradycardia.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION

items.question: Which of the following statements about pacemaker therapy in asymptomatic sinus bradycardia is correct?
options: Permanent pacing is typically indicated for asymptomatic sinus bradycardia.
Asymptomatic sinus bradycardia has been associated with adverse outcomes and warrants permanent pacing.
PPM is generally not indicated in asymptomatic sinus bradycardia without other symptoms.
Medications are the primary treatment to manage asymptomatic sinus bradycardia.
items.explanation: Asymptomatic sinus bradycardia does not typically warrant permanent pacing and is not associated with adverse outcomes.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION

items.question: Which of the following statements about the use of medications to improve heart rate in patients considering a pacemaker is correct?
options: Medications such as methylxanthines are frequently used long-term to avoid pacemaker implantation.
Beta agonists like terbutaline are commonly utilized for permanent management of bradycardia.
Oral theophylline may be considered temporarily to assess symptom improvement in sinus bradycardia.
Medications are rarely used, and when they are, it is typically on a long-term basis.
items.explanation: Oral theophylline may be used temporarily to determine if an increase in heart rate improves symptoms, suggesting PPM might be beneficial.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: TABLE 251-2 Indications for Permanent Pacing in Sinus Node Dysfunction (SND)
items.question: Which of the following is an indication for permanent pacing in patients with sinus node dysfunction?
options: Symptoms that are directly attributable to SND
Tachy-brady syndrome and symptoms not related to bradycardia
items.explanation: Permanent pacing is indicated for symptoms directly attributable to SND.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · TABLE 251-2 Indications for Permanent Pacing in Sinus Node Dysfunction (SND)

items.subtopic: TABLE 251-2 Indications for Permanent Pacing in Sinus Node Dysfunction (SND)
items.question: When might a trial of oral theophylline be considered in patients with sinus node dysfunction?
options: To determine if permanent pacing may be beneficial
As an alternative to leadless pacemakers
In all cases of SND without exception
items.explanation: Oral theophylline may be considered to increase heart rate and determine if permanent pacing is beneficial.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · TABLE 251-2 Indications for Permanent Pacing in Sinus Node Dysfunction (SND)

items.subtopic: TABLE 251-2 Indications for Permanent Pacing in Sinus Node Dysfunction (SND)
items.question: What does a 'D' indicate in the four-letter code used for pacing mode programming?
items.explanation: 'D' indicates dual chambers are paced in the four-letter code.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · TABLE 251-2 Indications for Permanent Pacing in Sinus Node Dysfunction (SND)

items.statement: Revascularization may sometimes reverse ischemia and alleviate bradycardia.
items.explanation: The passage states that revascularization can occasionally alleviate bradycardia by reversing ischemia.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · TEMPORARY PACING FOR TRANSIENT SUPPORT

items.statement: Sinus bradycardia after spinal cord injury is primarily due to enhanced sympathetic tone.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that the mechanism of bradycardia after spinal cord injury is enhanced parasympathetic tone, not sympathetic.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · TEMPORARY PACING FOR TRANSIENT SUPPORT

items.statement: There is a well-established heart rate below which pacemaker treatment is indicated.
items.explanation: No established heart rate threshold exists for indicating pacemaker treatment.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION

items.statement: Permanent pacing is commonly achieved through transvenous implantation of leads into cardiac chambers.
items.explanation: PPM is most commonly achieved via transvenous lead placement.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION

items.subtopic: TABLE 251-2 Indications for Permanent Pacing in Sinus Node Dysfunction (SND)
items.statement: Leadless pacemakers can be placed in both the right atrium and ventricle to provide dual chamber pacing.
items.explanation: Leadless pacemakers can indeed be placed in both chambers for dual pacing.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · TABLE 251-2 Indications for Permanent Pacing in Sinus Node Dysfunction (SND)

items.subtopic: TABLE 251-2 Indications for Permanent Pacing in Sinus Node Dysfunction (SND)
items.statement: The second letter in the four-letter code indicates the chamber(s) paced.
items.explanation: The second letter indicates the chamber(s) sensed, not paced.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · TABLE 251-2 Indications for Permanent Pacing in Sinus Node Dysfunction (SND)

items.assertion: Temporary pacing may be necessary in patients with sinus bradycardia after spinal cord injury.
items.reason: Enhanced parasympathetic tone and autonomic dysreflexia are common triggers for bradycardia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why temporary pacing may be necessary.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · TEMPORARY PACING FOR TRANSIENT SUPPORT

items.assertion: Permanent pacing is indicated for sleep-related pauses due to high vagal tone.
items.reason: Sleep-related pauses secondary to high vagal tone without other symptoms do not typically warrant permanent pacing.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; such cases generally do not require PPM.
items.reference: SA Nodal Disease · PERMANENT PACEMAKER IMPLANTATION
